title: Licensing & Governance
description: Open source licensing determines how code can be used, modified, and distributed. Governance models — DCO vs CLA, community charters, and compliance automation — establish the legal and social framework for project collaboration. This guide covers license selection, compatibility, sign-off workflows, and GitHub's governance tooling.

sections.title: License Types
sections.description:
Open source licenses fall into two categories: permissive and copyleft. Permissive licenses (MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD) allow anyone to use, modify, and distribute the code with minimal restrictions — even in proprietary software. Copyleft licenses (GPL, AGPL, LGPL) require derivative works to be distributed under the same license terms.

MIT — simplest, most permissive, widely adopted. Apache 2.0 — permissive with explicit patent grant. GPLv3 — strong copyleft, terminates patents grants. AGPLv3 — network copyleft, covers SaaS use. LGPL — weak copyleft, allows linking from proprietary code.

sections.title: DCO vs CLA
sections.description:
DCO (Developer Certificate of Origin) is a lightweight sign-off mechanism. Contributors add Signed-off-by: Name <email> to each commit, certifying they have the right to submit the code. DCO is automated via git commit -s and requires no legal review — it's the standard for Linux Foundation and CNCF projects.

CLA (Contributor License Agreement) is a separate legal document granting the project the right to use the contribution. CLAs are required by many corporate open source projects. The GitHub CLA Assistant bot automates CLA signing — it checks PRs for CLA status and blocks merge until signed.

sections.title: GitHub Governance
sections.description:
GitHub provides built-in governance tools: Repository roles (Read, Triage, Write, Maintain, Admin) with granular permission sets, CODEOWNERS for file-level responsibility, Branch protection rules for merge requirements, and Rulesets for org-wide policy enforcement.

For large projects, Teams with nested structures define maintainer hierarchies. GitHub Discussions serve as asynchronous governance forums — RFCs, ADRs, and community voting. Projects with custom workflows track governance tasks (e.g., license reviews, security audits).

sections.title: Compliance Automation
sections.description:
Automation ensures contributions comply with licensing and governance requirements. Key tools: CLA Assistant GitHub App (automated CLA signing and verification), DCO Probot (checks commits for Signed-off-by), REUSE.software (license compliance with SPDX headers), and FOSSA / Snyk for dependency license scanning.

GitHub Actions can enforce compliance: check for Signed-off-by in commits, verify CLA status via API, scan dependency licenses in CI, and block PRs that introduce incompatible licenses.

sections.title: License Compatibility
sections.description:
License compatibility determines whether code from differently-licensed projects can be combined. Key rules: Permissive licenses are compatible with each other and with copyleft (you can incorporate MIT code into a GPL project). Copyleft licenses are generally not compatible with proprietary or differently-termed copyleft licenses.

Common compatible pairs: MIT → Apache 2.0 → GPLv3 (one-way, can incorporate permissive into copyleft). Incompatible pairs: Apache 2.0 → GPLv2 (Apache's patent clause conflicts with GPLv2), GPLv3 → proprietary. Always verify using the choosealicense.com compatibility table.

details:
Licensing and governance are the legal and structural foundation of open source projects. Getting them right from the start prevents costly legal disputes and enables smooth community collaboration.

Choosing a License

Use https://choosealicense.com to select the right license. For projects seeking maximum adoption: MIT or Apache 2.0. For projects requiring contributions remain open: GPLv3. For SaaS projects requiring source disclosure: AGPLv3. No license means exclusive copyright — others cannot legally use, modify, or distribute the code.

DCO Details

The DCO text from developercertificate.org certifies four things: the contributor created or owns the contribution, the contribution was received from another DCO-certified project, or the contribution is available under a compatible open source license. The Signed-off-by trailer can be added with git commit -s or manually edited if forgotten. The DCO Probot checks all commits in a PR and reports missing sign-offs.

CLA Assistant Integration

Configure CLA Assistant in your repository's settings. Contributors sign once, and the CLA status is linked to their GitHub identity. The bot adds a check that passes or fails based on signature status. Custom CLA text is fully configurable — different CLAs for individuals vs corporations.

Repository Governance Maturity

As projects grow, governance evolves: Stage 1 (single maintainer, informal) → Stage 2 (core team, CODEOWNERS) → Stage 3 (foundation, legal entity, trademark). GitHub's Teams, Discussions, and Projects scale with each stage without requiring external tooling.

Code Examples

# DCO sign-off in commit message
Signed-off-by: Kallol Chakraborty <kallol@example.com>

# Git commit with automatic sign-off
git commit -s -m "feat: add OIDC authentication support"

# Check DCO compliance on existing commits
git log --format="%H %s%n%b" | grep -E "Signed-off-by:.*<.*>"

# SPDX license header for compliance
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
// Copyright (c) 2026 Kallol Chakraborty

-- GOVERNANCE.md structure --
# Governance
## Maintainers
- @kallol (core)
- @user2 (api)

## Decision Process
- RFCs via Discussions
- Consensus-based with lazy consensus
- 2-week comment period for major changes

## Contribution Process
1. Sign DCO with `git commit -s`
2. Open PR against `main`
3. At least 1 maintainer approval required
4. CI must pass before merge
cheatSheet:
Core Concepts

  • MIT / Apache 2.0 — permissive, maximises adoption. Apache 2.0 adds an explicit patent grant.
  • GPLv3 / AGPLv3 — copyleft, requires derivative works to stay open. AGPL covers SaaS.
  • DCO — lightweight sign-off via Signed-off-by in commit message. Standard for CNCF/Linux Foundation.
  • CLA — separate legal agreement, automated via CLA Assistant bot.

Staff+ Patterns

  • License compatibility flows one way: MIT → Apache 2.0 → GPLv3 (permissive can be absorbed by copyleft, not vice versa).
  • Use REUSE.software spec with SPDX headers for per-file license annotation — automated compliance.
  • GitHub's license detection API (GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/license) detects the license from the repo contents.

Watch Outs

  • No license = exclusive copyright by default. Others cannot legally use, modify, or distribute the code.
  • Apache 2.0 is incompatible with GPLv2 (Apache's patent termination clause conflicts with GPLv2's terms).
  • CLAs create friction for first-time contributors — DCO is preferred for community projects.

Interview Angle

Stripe L6: "Your startup uses a GPLv3 library — can you use it in your proprietary SaaS?" Answer: GPLv3 requires distribution of source to users. If the library is only used server-side and never distributed, GPLv3 doesn't trigger. But AGPLv3 covers network use. Know the AGPL distinction for SaaS scenarios.
